items.question_text: How should an equipment interchange flight be identified?

choices.text: Use the operating air carrier's company name in the aircraft identity.
Use the marketing carrier's company name, even if another carrier is operating the aircraft.
Use only the aircraft registration number and drop the company designator.
Use the destination airport identifier as the aircraft identity.

items.explanation: Equipment interchange flights are identified by the operating air carrier's company name.

items.question_text: Aircraft identity may use combinations that do not exceed seven alphanumeric characters.
items.explanation: The paragraph limits the aircraft-identity combinations to no more than seven alphanumeric characters.

items.question_text: Use the operating air carrier's company name when identifying equipment interchange flights.
items.explanation: The aircraft identity for an equipment interchange flight uses the operating carrier's company name.

items.situation: You need to state the identity of a Special Air Mission aircraft with the pronounceable-word call sign SAMP 316. Build the aircraft identity.

A controller enters an air carrier MEDEVAC flight as LAAL192 and says the letter L should always be used for any MEDEVAC mission. The same flight is also an equipment interchange operation flown by a different carrier than the one marketing the trip.
items.para_context: Civil aircraft identity may include the letter L for MEDEVAC in the registration-based civil format, but the letter L is not used for air carrier or air taxi MEDEVAC aircraft. Equipment interchange flights use the operating air carrier's company name in the aircraft identity.

choices.text: Do not use the L prefix for the air carrier MEDEVAC identity, and identify the flight with the operating air carrier's company name.
Keep the L prefix for any MEDEVAC mission and identify the flight with the marketing carrier's name instead of the operating carrier.
Replace the company identity with only the destination airport because interchange flights do not use carrier names.
Drop the aircraft identity entirely and record only the aircraft type and beacon code.

items.explanation: The L prefix is not used for air carrier or air taxi MEDEVAC aircraft, and equipment interchange flights use the operating carrier's company name.

items.question_text: How should aircraft type be recorded on flight progress data?

choices.text: Use the approved aircraft type designator.
Use any local nickname that controllers recognize.
Use the manufacturer's marketing name in plain language.
Use only the aircraft equipment suffix in place of aircraft type.

items.explanation: Aircraft type entries use the approved aircraft type designator.

items.question_text: Use the approved aircraft type designator in accordance with FAA Order 7360.1.
items.explanation: The paragraph directs controllers to use the approved aircraft type designator from FAA Order 7360.1.

items.question_text: Controllers must use the approved aircraft type designator for aircraft type entries.
items.explanation: Aircraft type entries are required to use the approved aircraft type designator.

items.question_text: The term approved aircraft type designator refers to the aircraft type designator listed in FAA Order 7360.1.
items.explanation: FAA Order 7360.1 is the governing source for approved aircraft type designators.

items.situation: A trainee wants to write a local nickname for an aircraft type on the strip because everyone in the room knows what it means. The approved FAA designator is available, but the trainee says the nickname is faster.
items.para_context: Aircraft type must be recorded with the approved aircraft type designator in accordance with FAA Order 7360.1.

choices.text: Use the approved aircraft type designator rather than the local nickname.
Use the local nickname if the sector already understands it.
Drop the aircraft type entirely and keep only the equipment suffix.
Use the aircraft registration number in place of the aircraft type designator.

items.explanation: The strip uses the approved aircraft type designator, not a local nickname or informal description.

items.question_text: How is a solo USAF or USN undergraduate student pilot identified in the flight plan?

choices.text: Add the letter Z as a suffix to the aircraft identification.
Prefix the aircraft identification with the letter Z on every transmission.
Add the letter L as a suffix to the aircraft identification.
Replace the aircraft identification with the word STUDENT.

items.explanation: Solo USAF or USN undergraduate student pilots are identified in the flight plan with the letter Z as a suffix.

items.question_text: Do not use the Z suffix in ground-to-air communication.
items.explanation: The Z suffix is a flight-plan identifier only and is not used in ground-to-air communication.

items.question_text: If the aircraft is piloted by a solo USAF or USN undergraduate student pilot, controllers must include the letter Z as a suffix in the flight plan identification.
items.explanation: That is the condition that triggers the Z suffix in the flight plan.

items.question_text: The Z suffix applies to identifying solo USAF or USN undergraduate student pilots in the flight plan.
items.explanation: The suffix applies to the flight-plan identification of those solo student pilots.

items.situation: A solo USAF undergraduate student pilot requests a revised clearance to avoid clouds in level flight. Another controller says that since the pilot has passed an instrument certification check, you can remove the Z suffix and use the suffix on the radio instead so everyone knows why the pilot is asking.
items.para_context: Solo USAF or USN undergraduate student pilots are identified in the flight plan with a Z suffix. The suffix is not used in ground-to-air communication. Even instrument-certified solo students may still request revised clearances to avoid clouds in level flight, and that does not change the requirement to use the Z suffix in the flight plan.

choices.text: Keep the Z suffix in the flight plan, do not use it on the radio, and expect revised clearances to avoid clouds in level flight if needed.
Remove the Z suffix after the student passes an instrument certification check and use the suffix on frequency instead.
Replace the Z suffix with L any time the student requests a weather-related change.
Deny all revised clearances because the Z suffix means the student may only operate in clear skies.

items.explanation: The Z suffix stays in the flight plan, is not spoken on the radio, and the note still allows revised clearances to avoid clouds in level flight.

items.question_text: How should an aircraft equipment suffix be changed?

choices.text: Modify the CNS equipment codes in the ICAO flight plan so automation generates the proper suffix.
Type a new slant suffix directly into the strip or data block.
Change the beacon code and let the suffix update itself from the transponder assignment.
Rewrite the aircraft type and leave the suffix unchanged.

items.explanation: Equipment suffix changes come from the CNS equipment codes, not direct suffix editing.

items.question_text: Automation uses the CNS equipment codes from the ICAO flight plan to generate the aircraft equipment suffix.
items.explanation: The suffix is automation-generated from the ICAO CNS equipment coding.

items.question_text: If using OFDPS or FDP2000, verbally coordinate equipment suffix changes with adjacent supported facilities.
items.explanation: Unsupported automation platforms require verbal coordination of suffix changes with adjacent supported facilities.

items.situation: You are forwarding suffix information for a GNSS-equipped Boeing 767. Build the required phraseology.

A trainee notices the suffix is wrong and wants to type a new slant suffix directly into the automation because it is faster. The strip is on an OFDPS platform, and the trainee is also unsure of the exact FDIO format needed to amend the equipment code properly.
items.para_context: Suffix changes are made by modifying the CNS equipment codes so automation generates the proper suffix. Directly changing the suffix can alter or delete other equipment codes. Unsupported OFDPS and FDP2000 platforms require verbal coordination with adjacent supported facilities, and if uncertain of the proper FDIO format, the change must be verbally coordinated with the appropriate en route facility.

choices.text: Do not type a direct slant-suffix change; coordinate the change verbally with the appropriate supported or en route facility and amend the equipment codes correctly.
Type the new suffix directly because the slant edit is isolated from the rest of the equipment coding.
Delete the suffix entirely and wait for the pilot to restate aircraft equipment on frequency.
Rewrite the aircraft type designator and leave the suffix alone because FDIO uncertainty is unrelated to equipment coding.

items.explanation: Unsupported platforms and uncertain FDIO formats both push the controller toward verbal coordination rather than direct suffix editing.

items.question_text: What symbol is placed at the clearance limit when holding instructions were included in the aircraft's original clearance?
items.explanation: H at the clearance limit shows holding instructions were included in the original clearance.

items.question_text: The term H at the clearance limit refers to holding instructions included in the aircraft's original clearance.
items.explanation: That is the meaning of the H clearance-status symbol at the clearance limit.

items.question_text: If delay is not anticipated, use F or O to indicate the clearance limit.
items.explanation: F or O at the clearance limit shows delay is not anticipated.

items.question_text: When holding differs from the established pattern for the fix, controllers must show detailed holding information after the dash.
items.explanation: Detailed turns, leg lengths, and other holding details follow the dash when the holding pattern differs from the established pattern.

items.situation: An aircraft was originally cleared to hold at the clearance limit, but the holding pattern now differs from the standard fix pattern. Another controller wants to leave only an H at the clearance limit because the aircraft is already delayed and the rest can be remembered verbally.
items.para_context: Use H at the clearance limit when holding instructions were included in the original clearance. When holding differs from the established pattern for the fix, show detailed holding information after the dash.

choices.text: Keep the H symbol and add the detailed holding information after the dash.
Use only the H symbol because holding details do not belong on the strip once delay is expected.
Replace the H with F because the aircraft is already in delay status.
Remove the clearance symbol entirely and rely on verbal memory for the holding details.

items.explanation: The H remains at the clearance limit, and nonstandard holding details are added after the dash.

items.question_text: Where may locally approved identifiers be used?

choices.text: Within your facility and on interphone circuits to adjacent facilities.
On teletypewriter circuits whenever the receiving facility already recognizes them.
Anywhere strip information is relayed, as long as a supervisor approves it verbally.

items.explanation: Locally approved identifiers stay inside the facility and are not used on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.

items.question_text: Locally approved identifiers may be used only within your facility and not on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.
items.explanation: The paragraph limits locally approved identifiers to internal facility use.

items.question_text: Control status of aircraft must always be current.
items.explanation: The paragraph states that aircraft control status must always be kept current.

items.question_text: In the clearance abbreviations table, what does CAF mean?
items.explanation: CAF in the clearance abbreviations table means Cleared as filed.

items.situation: A controller wants to use a locally approved identifier on an interphone line to an adjacent facility because it saves time. Another controller also suggests leaving an aircraft's control-status marking unchanged until the next strip update because everyone in the room already knows the aircraft was handed off.
items.para_context: Locally approved identifiers may be used only within your facility and not on teletypewriter or interphone circuits. Control status of aircraft must always be current.

choices.text: Do not use the local identifier on the interphone circuit, and update the aircraft's control status immediately so it remains current.
Use the local identifier if the adjacent facility is familiar with it, and delay the status update until the next strip change.
Use the local identifier only on interphone, because teletypewriter is the only prohibited circuit.
Keep the control status stale until the aircraft changes altitude or route, because that is when strip data matters most.

items.explanation: The local identifier stays inside the facility, and control status is not allowed to trail the actual state of the aircraft.

items.question_text: What is the maximum length of an aircraft identity combination under 2-3-5?
items.explanation: Aircraft identity combinations may not exceed seven alphanumeric characters.

items.question_text: Which company name is used to identify an equipment interchange flight?
items.explanation: Equipment interchange flights use the operating air carrier's company name.

choices.text: The operating air carrier's company name.

items.question_text: Which letter may be included in a civil aircraft identity to indicate air taxi?
items.explanation: The letter T may be used in the civil aircraft identity for air taxi.

items.question_text: The letter L is used for air carrier or air taxi MEDEVAC aircraft identities.
items.explanation: That note is the exception: the L is not used for air carrier or air taxi MEDEVAC aircraft.

items.question_text: What identifier is used for a commercial aircraft carrying the President?
items.explanation: The presidential commercial identifier in the table is EXEC1.

items.question_text: What source governs approved aircraft type designators?
items.explanation: Approved aircraft type designators are used in accordance with FAA Order 7360.1.

choices.text: FAA Order 7210.3, Flight Progress Strip Usage.
The AIM aircraft equipment suffix table.

items.question_text: How should aircraft type be entered on flight progress data?
items.explanation: The strip uses the approved aircraft type designator rather than a nickname or informal description.

choices.text: Use the approved aircraft type designator.
Use any local nickname widely understood in the facility.
Use only the equipment suffix if the aircraft is familiar.
Use the company's preferred marketing description.

items.question_text: Aircraft type entries must use the approved aircraft type designator.
items.explanation: That is the entire rule of 2-3-6.

items.question_text: Which reference must be followed for approved aircraft type designators?
items.explanation: The approved aircraft type designator comes from FAA Order 7360.1.

items.question_text: A local nickname may replace the approved aircraft type designator if the facility already understands the nickname.
items.explanation: The rule does not provide that exception; use the approved aircraft type designator.

items.question_text: What suffix identifies a solo USAF or USN undergraduate student pilot in the flight plan?
items.explanation: The flight plan uses the letter Z as the suffix.

items.question_text: Where is the Z suffix not used?
items.explanation: The Z suffix is not used in ground-to-air communication.

items.question_text: The Z suffix is removed after a solo USAF student pilot passes an instrument certification check.
items.explanation: The note says this does not change the requirement to use Z in the aircraft identification.

items.question_text: What revised clearance request may still be expected from these pilots even after instrument certification?
items.explanation: The note says revised clearances to avoid clouds in level flight can still be expected.

choices.text: A request to avoid clouds in level flight.
A request to cancel the Z suffix in the flight plan.
A request to use the Z suffix on the radio.
A request to be treated as an air carrier MEDEVAC flight.

items.question_text: Complete the required suffix for a solo undergraduate student pilot's flight plan identity: "... _____"
items.explanation: The identifying suffix is Z.

items.question_text: How is an aircraft equipment suffix changed under 2-3-8?
items.explanation: To change the suffix, amend the CNS equipment codes so automation translates them into the proper suffix.

choices.text: Modify the CNS equipment codes and let automation translate them into the proper suffix.
Type the new slant suffix directly into the strip.
Change the aircraft type designator and let the suffix follow automatically.
Have the pilot restate the suffix on frequency and record that spoken suffix directly.

items.question_text: Which suffixes identify GNSS-equipped aircraft under 2-3-8?
items.explanation: GNSS-equipped aircraft have equipment suffixes /G, /L, /S, or /V.

items.question_text: What should be done on OFDPS or FDP2000 when an equipment suffix change is needed?
items.explanation: Unsupported OFDPS and FDP2000 platforms require verbal coordination with adjacent supported facilities.

choices.text: Verbally coordinate the change with adjacent supported facilities.
Enter the new slant suffix directly because those platforms do not use CNS coding.
Delay the change until the next facility updates the beacon code.
Suppress the suffix entirely until a pilot confirms the route.

items.question_text: Directly changing the equipment suffix with a slant-prefixed symbol may unintentionally alter or delete other equipment codes.
items.explanation: That note is the reason direct suffix edits are avoided.

items.question_text: Complete the required suffix phraseology: "SEVEN-SIXTY-SEVEN SLANT _____."
items.explanation: The example for a GNSS-equipped 767 is Seven-Sixty-Seven slant Golf.

items.question_text: What symbol at the clearance limit shows holding instructions were included in the aircraft's original clearance?
items.explanation: The H symbol marks that original-clearance holding status.

items.question_text: What symbols indicate the clearance limit when a delay is not anticipated?
items.explanation: F or O indicate the clearance limit when delay is not anticipated.

items.question_text: When holding differs from the established pattern for the fix, what must be shown after the dash?
items.explanation: Detailed holding information such as turns and leg lengths is shown after the dash.

choices.text: Detailed holding information such as turns and leg lengths.
Only the next beacon code assignment.
Only the word DELAY without other details.

items.question_text: An H at the clearance limit is used only when holding differs from the established pattern.
items.explanation: H is tied to original-clearance holding instructions, not only to nonstandard patterns.

items.question_text: Which symbol is used at the clearance limit when holding instructions were included in the original clearance?
items.explanation: The symbol is H.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-10, where may locally approved identifiers be used?
items.explanation: Locally approved identifiers are used only within the facility and not on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.

choices.text: Within your facility and on interphone circuits.
On teletypewriter circuits if all facilities agree.
On any relay medium once the symbols are written on plain paper.

items.question_text: When flight progress strips are not used, what may be used to record information?
items.explanation: Plain sheets of paper or locally prepared forms may be used when strips are not used.

choices.text: Plain sheets of paper or locally prepared forms.
Only locally approved identifiers written from memory.
Only flight progress strips with abbreviations removed.

items.question_text: In the clearance abbreviations table, what does PD mean?
items.explanation: PD in the clearance abbreviations table means Cleared to climb or descend at pilot's discretion.

choices.text: Cleared to climb or descend at pilot's discretion.

items.question_text: Control status of aircraft must always be current.
items.explanation: That is the standing rule for control symbology and aircraft status marking.

items.question_text: Which clearance abbreviation means Cleared as filed?
items.explanation: CAF means Cleared as filed.
